mappings.rationaleAI: Directly addresses China from the republic through the communist revolution, Mao, Deng Xiaoping, and modern economic growth discussed throughout A.
Examines Maoist ideology, the role of peasants, the Great Leap Forward, the Cultural Revolution, and the Gang of Four in A.4.d and A.5.c-e.
Directly addresses the Great Proletarian Cultural Revolution (1966-76) including attacks on cultural leaders and party members in A.5.c.iii.
The Confucian heritage shaped Sun Yat-sen's political ideas and Chiang Kai-shek's idealization of Chinese tradition discussed in A.4.
Chinese literary culture is relevant to the drive for literature and arts for the masses during the Great Leap Forward in A.5.c.i.
Directly addresses Japan's constitutional government, militarism, WWII, postwar democratization, economic growth, and the Liberal-Democratic Party in B.
Japanese warrior culture provides background for the rise of militarism and the military's seizure of foreign policy initiative in B.2.a.
Japanese cultural and literary developments illuminate the postwar cultural changes in B.1.d.
Directly addresses Japanese rule (1910-45), the Korean War, and the development of both North and South Korea in C.
Examines Japan's war with China, the Greater East Asia Co-prosperity Sphere, the Pacific war, and Japan's surrender in A.3 and B.2.
The atomic bombing of Japanese cities and China's emergence as a nuclear power are discussed in A.5.c.ii and B.2.c.
Examines the fire and atomic bombing of Japanese cities and Allied air campaigns in the Pacific in B.2.c.
Explores the Sino-Soviet split, rapprochement with the U.S., the Korean War, and Cold War dynamics in East Asia throughout.
Explores the Chinese Communist Party, Maoist ideology, the Great Leap Forward, and the communist dimension of the Korean conflict in A and C.
Chinese nationalism, the May Fourth Movement, Japanese militarist nationalism, and Korean independence movements all shaped the region's modern history.
The Chinese Revolution, the Kuomintang-Communist struggle, and revolutionary transformations of Chinese society are central to A.
Addresses the Chinese Civil War (1945-49) and the Korean War in A.5.a and C.2.b.
Traces the Sino-Japanese War, the Pacific War, the Chinese Civil War, and the Korean War discussed throughout.
Explores the development of Kuomintang and Communist ideologies, Sun Yat-sen's three principles, and Maoist thought in A.4.
Examines authoritarian regimes including Chiang Kai-shek's Nationalist government, Mao's state, North Korean dictatorship, and KMT rule in Taiwan.
Sun Yat-sen's democratic ideals, the 1989 pro-democracy movement, Taiwan's gradual democratization, and South Korean democratic reforms are discussed in A.4-6 and C.2.c.
Addresses human-rights disputes between the U.S. and China, repression of the pro-democracy movement (1989), and political repression in North Korea.
Explores rapprochement between China and the U.S./Japan (1972), Sino-Soviet friction, and Korean armistice negotiations.
Japan's loss of colonies, Korean independence, and the end of Western colonial influence in East Asia are central themes.
Japan's economic miracle, China's market reforms and economic incentives, and South Korea's rapid development illustrate capitalism's varied forms.
Traces the economic transformations across East Asia including Japan's rise as a major world trader and China's rapid economic growth.
Geopolitical rivalries among China, Japan, the U.S., and the Soviet Union defined the region's modern trajectory.
Provides historical context for China's Central Asian connections relevant to Sino-Soviet relations and border tensions.
